From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

The **Church of Christ** was a [schismatic](/wiki/Schism_(religion) "Schism (religion)") sect from the [Latter Day Saint movement](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement") that was founded in 1848 by [James C. Brewster](/wiki/James_C._Brewster "James C. Brewster") and [Hazen Aldrich](/wiki/Hazen_Aldrich "Hazen Aldrich"). Because of the church's belief that Brewster was a [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet"), the group is often called the **Brewsterites**.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Church_of_Christ_(Brewsterite)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

After the [mob killing](/wiki/Killing_of_Joseph_Smith "Killing of Joseph Smith") of [Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith "Joseph Smith"), [apostle](/wiki/Apostle_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Apostle (Latter Day Saints)") [William E. McLellin](/wiki/William_E._McLellin "William E. McLellin") and other early church leaders returned to [Kirtland, Ohio](/wiki/Kirtland,_Ohio "Kirtland, Ohio"), to pick up the remnants of the church there. The group adopted the original name of the [Latter Day Saint church](/wiki/Church_of_Christ_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Church of Christ (Latter Day Saints)"), the "Church of Christ". Two principles of the movement were to (1) accept [David Whitmer](/wiki/David_Whitmer "David Whitmer") as leader, and (2) declare Kirtland as the proper center of the church.

At the organization of the church in [Springfield, Illinois](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"), [Hazen Aldrich](/wiki/Hazen_Aldrich "Hazen Aldrich") was selected as the organization's first president. He selected [James C. Brewster](/wiki/James_C._Brewster "James C. Brewster") and Jackson Goodale (1816–1896) as his counselors in the [First Presidency](/wiki/First_Presidency "First Presidency"). The church published a periodical known as the *Olive Branch*, which often contained [revelations](/wiki/Revelation_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Revelation (Latter Day Saints)") that Brewster had obtained. Notable adherents included [John E. Page](/wiki/John_E._Page "John E. Page"), one of the original members of the [Quorum of the Twelve](/wiki/Quorum_of_the_Twelve "Quorum of the Twelve").

Because Brewster lived in Springfield, the newspaper eventually moved operation to Illinois. In October 1849, a newspaper article indicated that Kirtland was a temporary headquarters, and they planned to move to California. On June 23, 1849, the group held a General Assembly in the [Kirtland Temple](/wiki/Kirtland_Temple "Kirtland Temple"). Six members accepted Brewster's revelation to move to California.

In 1850, Brewster declared that there was a land called "Bashan" in the [Rio Grande Valley](/wiki/Lower_Rio_Grande_Valley "Lower Rio Grande Valley") that God had selected as the new gathering place for the church. In 1851, Brewster and Goodale led a wagon train of followers to find Bashan, while Aldrich—who had begun to doubt Brewster's prophetic abilities—remained behind in Kirtland.

There were disagreements between Brewster and Goodale and the other members of the church on the journey to Bashan, and most of Brewster's followers, including [Olive Oatman](/wiki/Olive_Oatman "Olive Oatman") and her family, deserted Brewster and headed for [California](/wiki/California "California"). Brewster returned to Illinois and the church was never reorganized.
